# Industrial Control Panels Relating To Hazardous Locations

cULus Category NRBX/NRBX7

General

For electrical devices used in a hazardous location, it is necessary to prevent ignition of the hazardous material by a spark or high temperature. One method to prevent ignition, called intrinsic safety, is to strictly limit the available energy.

This energy limitation is accomplished, in part, by the use of a device called an intrinsic safety barrier. This "barrier" will, by the use of specific electrical components, prevent excessive voltage, current, and power downstream.

It is also necessary to consider the devices and wiring connected to the barrier as these can affect the energy available in the hazardous location. A proper intrinsic safety evaluation considers the combination of the intrinsic safety barrier, wiring and intrinsically safe device.

Slide 2

General, continued

■ An industrial control panel which contains an intrinsic safety barrier (limiting the energy in the circuit) is a NRBX/NRBX7 panel.

■ The NRBX panel does not need to be in a hazardous location (NITW).

■ Or, the NRBX panel may be in the hazardous location which must have protection against ignition of the hazardous material by another means (NNNY).

■ The intrinsically safe wiring may extend to as long as several miles/kilometers or as short as the cover of the panel, such as for a panel mounted switch (field wiring of zero length).

GOING Global ... with Knowledge Solutions

UL Underwriters
Laboratories Inc.

NITW

NITW
and
NRBX

•Safe area installation

NITW
and
NNNY

NITW,
NRBX
and
NNNY

•Hazardous area installation

•Non-I.S.

•I.S. connections

■ Panel program NNNY for Hazardous
Locations requires NITW as prerequisite.

■ Panel Program NRBX for Intrinsically Safe
circuit extensions also requires NITW as
prerequisite.

General Use

■ Equipment that is intended to be connected to a barrier in the panel, can be simple apparatus. Simple apparatus includes LED's, resistors, RTD's, switches, and thermocouples that do not generate more than 1.2 V, 0.1 A, 25 mW, and do not dissipate more than 1.3 Watts. They do not require any listing or labelling.

■ Equipment that is intended to be connected to a barrier in the panel may be more than simple apparatus (for example a transmitter providing 4-20 mA current loop) provided the device is listed as intrinsically safe and is installed in accordance with all applicable instructions. These instructions are commonly call control drawings.

Slide 5

Making the connections safely

■ Panel manufacturer are required to use barrier(s) providing outputs suitable for the specified environment and to include barrier manufacturer's Control Drawing with Installation Instructions furnished by panel manufacturer.

■ When device connected is not a simple apparatus, it is necessary for the entity parameters between the barrier and the device match the following:

I.S. Apparatus
Entity Parameters | Required Relationship Between Entity Parameters | UL Listed I.S. Barrier Entity Parameters
---|---|---
V max (or Ui) | ≥ | Voc or Vt (or Uo)
I max (or Ii) | ≥ | Isc or It (or Io)
P max, Pi | ≥ | Po
Ci + Ccable | ≤ | Ca (or Co)
Li + Lcable | ≤ | La (or Lo)

■ Unless known otherwise, cable contains 60 pF/ft and 20 uH/ft.

Slide 6

Intrinsically Safe/Non-Intrinsically Safe Separations

Separations between intrinsically safe and non-intrinsically safe wiring and circuits may be maintained by:

• Distance (50 mm between intrinsically safe and non-intrinsically safe, including slack for field wiring)

• The slack for field wiring is an installation, not a construction concern but typically (see the NEC and local codes) is considered to be six inches. This results in the "2-5-8 inches" rule of thumb commonly used in the industry. Do not build a panel that cannot be properly installed!

• Partitions (0.45 mm metal or 0.9 mm plastic) where distance is not adequately maintained.

Slide 7

# Separation accomplished by partition

Does the partition need to extend all the way to the cover (within 1.5 mm)?

Slide 9

Wiring must be identified (marked) both inside and outside the panel

INTRINSIC SAFE WIRING

NON-INTRINSIC SAFE WIRING

Slide 10

Common mistakes in NRBX constructions

1) Any barrier which is not UL listed is not acceptable.

2) Partitions which do not sufficiently protect the wiring

3) Wires that are not protected by partitions or distances

4) Intrinsically Safe connections are not marked inside panel

5) Intrinsically Safe connections are not marked outside panel

6) No control drawing (wiring instructions)

7) Label does not includes a reference to the control drawing

Slide 12
